A vent coughs sparks and sulfur before tiny molten shapes scramble out laughing. They dive back into cracks whenever struck.
A glowing serpent coils across black stones where heat shimmers in the air. It lashes out when its basking ground is disturbed.
Hammer blows echo from beyond a curtain of heat. The azer patrol steps through the shimmer, more territorial than curious.
The black shelf looks solid, but red heat glows in the cracks. Reading the crust reveals which parts can bear weight.
A warm wind drops gray ash thick enough to blind and choke. Cloth over the mouth helps, but grit still scrapes eyes and lungs.
A quiet fumarole erupts in a shriek of steam. A fast leap keeps scalding vapor from finding every gap in armor.
An obsidian shrine has cracked from base to crown, leaking warm red light. Someone needs the offering chamber checked before the next tremor.
A prospector's marker flag flaps beside a new lava tube. Their bootprints enter the opening and do not come out.
A bell half buried in ash rings whenever the ground trembles. Digging it free may reveal what settlement once stood here.
Bright fireweed grows in the first soil to return after a burn. The young shoots are edible if cut before ash coats them.
Yellow moss clings to warm stones near a fumarole. It is useful in small amounts, but the fumes punish slow work.
Heat has opened hard seed cones in a scorched grove. The seeds can be shaken free before wind scatters them across the ash.
A warm spring runs clear between black stones, full of minnows adapted to the heat. They scatter into narrow cracks when shadows pass.
Eels coil in a dark lake rimmed with ash and steam. The water is warm enough to sting after a long reach.
A cold crater pool hides trout beneath a skin of floating ash. Clearing the surface quietly is the challenge.
A glossy obsidian flow has fractured into sharp plates. Good pieces can be wrapped and lifted without slicing packs or palms.
Yellow crystals grow around a steaming vent in fragile clusters. The useful pieces must be taken between breaths of hot gas.
A fall of basalt columns lies where a cliff cooled and cracked. Straight sections can be worked free with wedges.
A burned tree has fallen in one piece, its heart charred into useful fuel. Cutting too hard turns it to useless dust.
A pine killed by heat still stands dry and resin-rich at the edge of the flow. Dropping it safely requires watching the wind.
The remains of an old shelter poke from the ash, its beams protected from weather by the fall. Digging them free sends hot dust everywhere.
